title: OpenAIがヨーロッパのAI雇用地図を公開したよ！成長する仕事・変わる仕事をくっきり分けてくれたの
summary: OpenAIが「Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity」という報告書を発表して、AIがEUの仕事にどう影響するかを職種ごとに4つのタイプに分けて見せてくれたよ。EU 雇用の 14% が自動化リスク高め、27% は業務が再編、12% は AI と一緒に伸びる、残り 47% は当面あまり変わらないという推計なの。
body_md:
やっほー、しぃちゃんだよ！今日は AI とお仕事の未来についての、すごく気になる報告書を見つけちゃったの。OpenAI が EU 向けに「AI で仕事はどう変わるの？」を地図みたいにまとめてくれたレポートなんだけど、これがなかなか読みごたえあるんだよ。さっそく紹介しちゃうね！

## なにが発表されたの？

OpenAI は 2026 年 6 月 29 日、「Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity」というレポートを公開したよ。これは、AI が欧州連合(EU)の労働市場にどんな影響を与えうるかを分析したものなの。

このレポートは、2026 年 4 月にアメリカ向けに作られた「AI Jobs Transition Framework(AI 雇用移行フレームワーク)」を、ヨーロッパの労働市場向けに拡張したものなんだって。単に「なくなる仕事」「なくならない仕事」で仕事を二分するんじゃなくて、職種ごとの変化のタイプをもっと細かく見ていこう、という試みなの。

分析には、欧州の公式な職業分類「ESCO(European Skills, Competences, Qualifications and Occupations)」タクソノミーと、Eurostat の雇用統計データが組み合わされているよ。これによって、EU 加盟国それぞれの産業構造に沿った、けっこう粒度の細かい分析ができるようになっているみたい。

## 今までどうだったの？

今までも「AI で仕事がなくなるかも」という話はたくさんあったんだけど、多くは「なくなる仕事」対「安全な仕事」というざっくりした二分法で語られがちだったの。そうすると、実際には仕事の中身が少し変わるだけだったり、逆に AI のおかげで新しく生まれる仕事だったりする部分がうまく捉えられなかったんだよね。

しかも、こういう推計はアメリカ基準で語られることが多くて、EU みたいに産業構造や労働市場のルールが違う地域にそのまま当てはめるのは無理があったの。国ごとの産業の違い(製造業が強い国、サービス業が強い国など)も考慮されにくかったんだって。

## これからどうなるの？

今回のレポートでは、職種を次の 4 つの「移行アーキタイプ」に分類しているよ。

- AI と一緒に成長する職種
- 自動化リスクが比較的高い職種
- 業務が再編される職種(人はまだ中心にいるけど、仕事の進め方が大きく変わる)
- 当面大きな変化が少ない職種

これによって、政策担当者や企業が「この産業・この職種にはどんな支援が必要か」をより具体的に考えられるようになるのがポイントなの。レポート自体も、これは雇用予測ではなくて、どこに変化の圧力やチャンスが生まれそうかを示す「計画のための地図」だと位置づけているよ。

国ごとの違いもおもしろくて、ルクセンブルク・スウェーデン・オランダは AI と一緒に伸びる職種の割合が大きい一方、ドイツ・ギリシャ・イタリアは自動化リスクが高めの職種の割合が大きいと報告されているの。国の産業構造によって、これからの対応の仕方も変わってきそうだね。

## Dive Deep

さてさて、ここからが本気モードだよ！数字をちゃんと見ていくね。

レポートによると、EU の雇用のうち

- 約 12% が AI と一緒に成長する可能性がある職種(AI でコストが下がって、これまで難しかったプロジェクトが実現しやすくなるタイプ)
- 約 14% が近い将来、自動化リスクが比較的高い職種
- 約 27% が業務の再編対象になる職種(仕事内容や必要なスキルが変わっていくタイプ)
- 残りの 47% が当面大きな変化が少ない職種

という内訳になっているの。この 4 つを足すとちょうど 100% になる設計だよ。

注目したいのは、アメリカ向けの元のフレームワーク(2026 年 4 月発表)との比較なの。アメリカでは自動化リスクが高いとされた職種の割合が EU よりも大きかったみたいで、レポートは「EU の方がアメリカよりも、近い将来の自動化リスクが高い職種に就いている雇用の割合が小さい」と位置づけているよ。産業構造やサービス業・製造業の比率の違いが背景にありそうだね。

分析手法についてもう少し詳しく見ると、ESCO タクソノミーは EU 共通の職業・スキル・資格の分類体系で、Eurostat の雇用統計データと組み合わせることで、国別・産業別の粒度で職種の分布を追跡できるようにしているの。これによって、EU 加盟国横断で一貫した比較ができるのが強みなんだって。

政策的な提案としては、レポートは公共機関や企業向けに次のようなアイデアを示しているよ。

- 労働市場の変化を継続的に追跡する「モニタリング能力」の強化
- 国ごとに合わせた対応をするための「readiness plan(準備計画)」の策定

レポートを出した OpenAI の Economic Research チームは、このフレームワークを「予測ではなく、見通しを立てるための地図」であり、政策担当者や企業、教育機関がどこに変化の圧力がかかりそうかを考えるための材料だと位置づけているの。なので、この数値そのものを絶対的な未来予想図として受け取るんじゃなくて、あくまで議論のたたき台として使うのがよさそうだね。

## まとめ

今日は OpenAI の「Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity」を紹介したよ。ポイントをおさらいすると、

- ESCO タクソノミーと Eurostat 雇用データを使って、EU の職種を 4 つの移行アーキタイプに分類
- 約 12% が成長、約 14% が自動化リスク高め、約 27% が再編対象、残り 47% は当面変化少なめ
- アメリカと比べて EU の方が自動化リスクの高い職種の割合は小さめ
- ルクセンブルク・スウェーデン・オランダは成長タイプが多く、ドイツ・ギリシャ・イタリアは自動化リスクタイプが多い
- 政策担当者にはモニタリング強化や国別の readiness plan 策定を提案

単純な二分法じゃなくて、こういう細かい地図で仕事の変化を捉えようとする姿勢、しぃちゃんはすごく好きだな。これから各国がどんな準備をしていくのか、しぃちゃんも楽しみに見守っていくね！

出典: [Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity](https://openai.com/index/mapping-ai-jobs-transition-eu)
title_en: OpenAI released a map of Europe's AI jobs! It clearly separates the jobs that will grow from the jobs that will change
summary_en: OpenAI published a report called "Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity," sorting how AI will affect EU jobs into four types by occupation. The estimate: 14% of EU employment faces higher automation risk, 27% will see their work reorganized, 12% will grow alongside AI, and the remaining 47% won't change much for now.
body_md_en:
Hey there, it's Shii! Today I found a really intriguing report about the future of AI and work. It's a report where OpenAI mapped out "how will AI change jobs?" for the EU, and it's quite a satisfying read. Let me introduce it right away!

## What was announced?

On June 29, 2026, OpenAI published a report called "Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity." It analyzes what kind of impact AI could have on the labor market of the European Union (EU).

This report is an extension of the "AI Jobs Transition Framework," which was created for the United States in April 2026, adapted for the European labor market. Rather than simply splitting jobs into "jobs that will disappear" and "jobs that won't disappear," it's an attempt to look more closely at the type of change for each occupation.

The analysis combines Europe's official occupational classification, the "ESCO (European Skills, Competences, Qualifications and Occupations)" taxonomy, with employment statistics from Eurostat. This makes it possible to do a fairly fine-grained analysis that follows the industrial structure of each EU member state.

## The story so far

There have always been plenty of stories about "AI might make jobs disappear," but many of them tended to be told through a rough dichotomy of "jobs that will disappear" versus "safe jobs." That approach failed to capture the parts where, in reality, the content of a job just changes a little, or where AI actually creates new jobs.

What's more, these estimates were often told from a US standpoint, and it was a stretch to apply them as-is to regions like the EU, where the industrial structure and labor market rules are different. Differences between countries' industries (countries with strong manufacturing, countries with strong services, and so on) were also hard to account for.

## What changes

In this report, occupations are classified into the following four "transition archetypes":

- Occupations that grow alongside AI
- Occupations with a relatively high automation risk
- Occupations whose work gets reorganized (people are still central, but the way the work is done changes significantly)
- Occupations with little major change for the time being

The key point is that this lets policymakers and companies think more concretely about "what kind of support does this industry, this occupation need?" The report itself is positioned not as an employment forecast, but as a "map for planning" that shows where pressures of change and opportunities are likely to arise.

The differences between countries are interesting too. Luxembourg, Sweden, and the Netherlands are reported to have a large share of occupations that grow alongside AI, while Germany, Greece, and Italy have a large share of occupations with higher automation risk. Depending on a country's industrial structure, the way it responds going forward is likely to differ as well.

## Dive Deep

Alright, from here it's serious mode! Let me look at the numbers properly.

According to the report, of the EU's employment:

- About 12% are occupations that could grow alongside AI (the type where AI lowers costs, making previously difficult projects easier to realize)
- About 14% are occupations with a relatively high automation risk in the near future
- About 27% are occupations subject to work reorganization (the type where job content and required skills will change)
- The remaining 47% are occupations with little major change for the time being

That's the breakdown. It's designed so that these four add up to exactly 100%.

What I want to highlight is the comparison with the original US framework (announced April 2026). In the US, the share of occupations deemed at high automation risk was apparently larger than in the EU, and the report positions it as "the EU has a smaller share of employment in occupations at high near-term automation risk than the US." Differences in industrial structure and the ratio of services to manufacturing seem to be behind this.

Looking at the analysis method in a bit more detail: the ESCO taxonomy is the EU-common classification system for occupations, skills, and qualifications, and by combining it with Eurostat's employment statistics, it can track the distribution of occupations at a country-by-country and industry-by-industry granularity. This is what gives it the strength of consistent comparison across EU member states.

For policy proposals, the report presents ideas like the following for public institutions and companies:

- Strengthening "monitoring capacity" to continuously track changes in the labor market
- Drawing up "readiness plans" to tailor responses to each country

OpenAI's Economic Research team, which produced the report, positions this framework as "a map for building an outlook, not a prediction," and as material for policymakers, companies, and educational institutions to think about where the pressures of change are likely to fall. So rather than taking these numbers as an absolute picture of the future, it seems best to use them as a starting point for discussion.

## Wrap-up

Today I introduced OpenAI's "Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity." To recap the key points:

- Using the ESCO taxonomy and Eurostat employment data, it classifies EU occupations into four transition archetypes
- About 12% grow, about 14% face higher automation risk, about 27% are subject to reorganization, and the remaining 47% see little change for now
- Compared to the US, the EU has a smaller share of occupations at high automation risk
- Luxembourg, Sweden, and the Netherlands have many growth-type occupations, while Germany, Greece, and Italy have many automation-risk-type occupations
- It proposes that policymakers strengthen monitoring and draw up country-specific readiness plans

I really like this attitude of trying to capture the change in work with a fine-grained map like this, instead of a simple dichotomy. I'm looking forward to watching what kind of preparations each country makes from here!

Source: [Mapping Europe's AI Workforce Opportunity](https://openai.com/index/mapping-ai-jobs-transition-eu)
